task.title: Configuring a WireGuard server by using the RHEL web console

li.p: You are logged in to the RHEL web console.
You know the following information:
The static tunnel IP addresses and subnet masks of both the server and client
The public key of the client

context.p: You can configure a WireGuard server by using the browser-based RHEL web console. Use this method to let NetworkManager manage the WireGuard connection.

step.cmd: Select the Networking tab in the navigation on the left side of the screen.
Click Add VPN in the Interfaces section.
If the wireguard-tools and systemd-resolved packages are not already installed, the web console displays a corresponding notification. Click Install to install these packages.
Enter the name of the WireGuard device that you want to create.
Configure the key pair of this host:

li.p: If you want use the keys that the web console has created:
Keep the pre-selected Generated option in the Private key area.
Note the Public key value. You require this information when you configure the client.
If you want to use an existing private key:
Select Paste existing key in the Private key area.
Paste the private key into the text field. The web console automatically calculates the corresponding public key.

step.cmd: Set a listen port number, such as 51820, for incoming WireGuard connections.

info.p: Always set a fixed port number on hosts that receive incoming WireGuard connections. If you do not set a port, WireGuard uses a random free port each time you activate the interface.

step.cmd: Set the tunnel IPv4 address and subnet mask of the server.

info.p: To also set an IPv6 address, you must edit the connection after you have created it.

step.cmd: Add peer configurations for each client that you want to allow to communicate with this server:

li.p: Click Add peer.
Enter the public key of the client.
Leave the Endpoint field empty.
Set the Allowed IPs field to the tunnel IP addresses of the clients that are allowed to send data to this server.

step.cmd: Click Add to create the WireGuard connection.
If you want to also set a tunnel IPv6 address:

li.p: Click on the WireGuard connection’s name in the Interfaces section.
Click edit next to IPv6.
Set the Addresses field to Manual, and enter the tunnel IPv6 address and prefix of the server.
Click Save.

step.cmd: If you use this host in a network with DHCP or Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC), the connection can be vulnerable to being redirected. For details and mitigation steps, see Assigning a VPN connection to a dedicated routing table to prevent the connection from bypassing the tunnel.

li.p: Display the interface configuration of the wg0 device:
li.codeblock:
# wg show wg0 interface: wg0 public key: UtjqCJ57DeAscYKRfp7cFGiQqdONRn69u249Fa4O6BE= private key: (hidden) listening port: 51820
peer: bnwfQcC8/g2i4vvEqcRUM2e6Hi3Nskk6G9t4r26nFVM= allowed ips: 192.0.2.2/32, 2001:db8:1::2/128
li.p: To display the private key in the output, use the WG_HIDE_KEYS=never wg show wg0 command.
Display the IP configuration of the wg0 device:
li.codeblock: # ip address show wg0 20: wg0: <POINTOPOINT,NOARP,UP,LOWERUP> mtu 1420 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000 link/none inet 192.0.2.1/24 brd 192.0.2.255 scope global noprefixroute wg0 valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever inet6 2001:db8:1::1/32 scope global noprefixroute valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever inet6 fe80::3ef:8863:1ce2:844/64 scope link noprefixroute valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

li.p: Configure the firewalld service on the WireGuard server.
